options.rationale: IV adrenaline boluses risk arrhythmia and myocardial ischaemia; infusion (not bolus) is the escalation route after IM doses per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

options.text: Give nebulised salbutamol 5 mg as primary treatment for the wheeze and hold further adrenaline
options.rationale: Nebulised salbutamol is adjunctive for bronchospasm in anaphylaxis but does not replace IM adrenaline, which addresses upper airway oedema, bronchospasm and hypotension per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

options.text: Give an additional dose of oral cetirizine 10 mg and observe
options.rationale: Antihistamines do not treat the airway or circulatory features of anaphylaxis and are adjunctive only per ASCIA Anaphylaxis Guidelines.

explanation.summary: Anaphylaxis is treated with prompt IM adrenaline 10 microg/kg (max 500 microg) into the lateral thigh, repeated every 5 minutes as needed. Failure to respond to two IM doses warrants a third IM dose and an IV crystalloid bolus, then an IV adrenaline infusion. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjunctive only.
key_points: First-line: IM adrenaline 10 microg/kg lateral thigh
Refractory anaphylaxis: repeat IM adrenaline, IV fluid bolus, then IV adrenaline infusion
Adjuncts: oxygen, salbutamol for bronchospasm, antihistamines for rash
Observe at least 4 hours after stabilisation (longer if biphasic risk)
Discharge with EpiPen prescription, ASCIA Action Plan, and allergist referral
explanation.pearls: Beta-blocker therapy can blunt the adrenaline response; consider IV glucagon 20-30 microg/kg in refractory cases on a beta-blocker.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is found unresponsive in the playground at his childcare centre. The childcare nurse confirms he is not breathing normally despite a 10-second look-listen-feel assessment. There is no obvious chest movement and no audible breath sounds. An ambulance has been called. The nurse asks the bystander medical student to commence basic life support immediately while she retrieves the AED from the office. The child has no known medical history, is not known to childcare staff to have any allergies, and there is no witnessed foreign body event. The student is alone with the patient for the next 60 seconds until the nurse returns. She has been trained in ANZCOR paediatric BLS.

data_table.Vital signs: Unresponsive, not breathing normally, pulse not yet assessed
data_table.Examination: Apnoea, no chest movement, no obvious cyanosis yet, no visible trauma, no obvious foreign body in mouth on quick visual check

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate compression-to-ventilation ratio in single-rescuer paediatric BLS?

options.text: 15 compressions to 2 breaths at a rate of approximately 100-120 compressions per minute
options.rationale: 15:2 is the two-rescuer paediatric ratio used by trained healthcare providers; single-rescuer ratio is different per ANZCOR Guideline 12.

options.text: 30 compressions to 2 breaths at a rate of approximately 100-120 compressions per minute
options.rationale: Single-rescuer paediatric BLS uses 30:2 (the same as adult BLS) at 100-120 compressions per minute and a compression depth of approximately one third of chest AP diameter, per ANZCOR Guideline 12.

options.text: 5 compressions to 1 breath at a rate of approximately 80 compressions per minute
options.rationale: This ratio is not used in current ANZCOR algorithms; 5:1 is historical and replaced by 30:2 single-rescuer per ANZCOR Guideline 12.

options.text: Continuous chest compressions only with no ventilations until paramedics arrive
options.rationale: Paediatric arrests are usually respiratory in origin; ventilations are essential and compression-only CPR is reserved for adult bystander CPR per ANZCOR Guideline 12.

options.text: 10 compressions to 2 breaths at a rate of approximately 60 compressions per minute
options.rationale: Neither the ratio nor the rate matches ANZCOR paediatric BLS guidance; rate is 100-120 per minute per ANZCOR Guideline 12.

explanation.summary: ANZCOR paediatric BLS uses 30:2 for single-rescuer (lay or healthcare) and 15:2 for two-rescuer healthcare providers. Compression rate is 100-120 per minute, depth is one third of chest AP diameter, with full chest recoil and minimal interruption. Attach the AED as soon as available.
key_points: Single-rescuer paediatric: 30:2
Two-rescuer healthcare provider paediatric: 15:2
Rate 100-120/min, depth one third AP chest diameter
Use paediatric AED pads where available for under 8 years; if not, use adult pads
Paediatric arrests are usually respiratory; ventilations matter
explanation.pearls: If unsure whether to use paediatric or adult AED pads, use whatever you have; doing nothing is worse.

stem: A 3-year-old previously well boy is brought to the GP by his mother with a 6-month history of infrequent painful bowel motions, on average every 4-5 days, with hard pellet-like or wide stools that sometimes block the toilet. There has been occasional faecal soiling between motions for the past 2 months. He passed meconium at 18 hours after birth and had no neonatal feeding concerns. He is fully toilet-trained for urine and was day-trained for stool by age 2.5 but recently has been withholding. His diet is varied but low in fibre and high in cow's milk (around 700 mL per day). There is no abdominal distension when well, no vomiting, no failure to thrive, weight tracking along the 50th centile, and no family history of Hirschsprung disease. He has no perianal lesions and no neurological signs.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8 deg C, HR 100 bpm, RR 22 /min, BP 100/60 mmHg
data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, palpable faecal mass in the left iliac fossa, no peritonism, normal anal position, no anal fissure, normal anal tone, normal lower limb tone and reflexes, growth on 50th centile
data_table.Investigations: Bedside abdominal examination only; UEC and TFTs not indicated by history

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Refer urgently to paediatric surgery for rectal biopsy to exclude Hirschsprung disease
options.rationale: Hirschsprung is unlikely given normal meconium passage, no failure to thrive, normal exam, and onset in toddler age with withholding; functional constipation is far more likely per RCH CPG: Constipation.

options.text: Disimpact with oral macrogol 3350 (e.g. Movicol Junior) escalating dose schedule over 3-7 days, then commence maintenance macrogol with toileting routine, dietary advice, and review in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Functional constipation with a palpable faecal mass is managed with oral macrogol disimpaction followed by maintenance, plus behavioural toileting routine and dietary modification per RCH CPG: Constipation and NICE NG99.

options.text: Commence regular phosphate enemas weekly for 2 months as first-line treatment
options.rationale: Oral macrogol disimpaction is preferred over enemas, which are reserved for failed oral disimpaction or impacted patients in whom oral therapy is impractical per RCH CPG: Constipation.

options.text: Restrict all dairy products and review in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Excess cow's milk is a contributing factor but full restriction is not first-line; moderation plus disimpaction and maintenance laxatives are recommended per RCH CPG: Constipation.

options.text: Reassure that bowel actions every 4-5 days are within the normal range and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Soiling, painful defecation and a palpable faecal mass meet criteria for functional constipation and require active treatment, not reassurance, per RCH CPG: Constipation.

explanation.summary: Functional constipation in toddlers is managed with oral macrogol disimpaction over several days followed by maintenance laxative therapy, toileting routine (sit-after-meals, footrest), and dietary advice (fibre, water, moderate dairy). Investigations are reserved for red flags suggesting Hirschsprung disease or organic causes.
key_points: Red flags for Hirschsprung: delayed meconium > 48 h, failure to thrive, ribbon stools from birth, palpable empty rectum on PR
Macrogol is first-line for disimpaction and maintenance
Enemas are second-line when oral fails
Treat for 6-12 months and wean slowly to prevent relapse
explanation.pearls: Reflex anal-cutaneous response (anal wink) and lower-limb neurology screen exclude tethered cord; document them.

stem: A 14-month-old girl is brought to the GP because her mother is concerned she is not gaining weight. She was born at 38 weeks weighing 3.0 kg (25th centile). At 6 months she weighed 7.2 kg (25th centile). She now weighs 8.0 kg, which has fallen across two major weight centile lines and is now below the 3rd centile. Her length and head circumference remain on the 25th centile. She is exclusively on family foods plus 600 mL of cow's milk per day; her mother reports prolonged mealtimes with frequent refusal of solids. She has no vomiting, no diarrhoea, no respiratory symptoms, and no fevers. She is fully immunised. The family lives in stable housing with no documented family violence. Developmentally she walks holding furniture, says two words, and points. There is no family history of coeliac disease or cystic fibrosis. She passed her newborn screen.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.7 deg C, HR 115 bpm, RR 26 /min, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Alert and engaged, weight < 3rd centile having crossed 2 major centile lines, length on 25th centile, head circumference 25th centile, no dysmorphic features, mildly reduced subcutaneous fat over buttocks, abdomen soft, no organomegaly
data_table.Investigations: Office observation; no bloods done yet; growth chart available since birth
data_table.History: Cow's milk 600 mL/day, prolonged mealtimes, food refusal, no GI symptoms, normal development apart from feeding

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Admit for inpatient nasogastric feeding and full workup
options.rationale: Inpatient NG feeding is reserved for severe faltering growth with safeguarding concerns, failure of community management, or severe malnutrition; outpatient assessment is first-line here per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive.

options.text: Take a detailed feeding and dietary history, review the growth chart, perform a baseline screening blood panel (FBC, UEC, LFTs, coeliac serology, iron studies, TFTs) and urinalysis, reduce cow's milk to under 500 mL/day, and arrange dietitian and paediatric outpatient review
options.rationale: Faltering growth crossing two centiles is investigated with a structured history, growth-chart review, a focused screening panel, dietary modification (limit cow's milk to under 500 mL/day to prevent iron deficiency and energy displacement), and multidisciplinary outpatient review per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive and NICE NG75.

options.text: Reassure as normal toddler variation and review in 6 months
options.rationale: Falling across 2 major weight centiles is significant faltering growth requiring assessment, not delayed review, per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive.

options.text: Commence high-calorie formula supplements without dietary or medical assessment
options.rationale: Empirical formula supplementation without identifying cause and engaging dietitians is not best practice; the underlying contribution (excess cow's milk, behavioural feeding pattern) must be addressed per NICE NG75.

options.text: Refer immediately to child protection given concern for neglect
options.rationale: Without specific safeguarding red flags, the first step is medical and dietetic assessment; child protection referral is reserved for cases with neglect features per RCH CPG: Failure to thrive.

explanation.summary: Faltering growth is assessed with a structured history (feeding behaviour, intake, GI symptoms, developmental, social), thorough examination, growth chart review across centiles, a focused screening panel, and dietary modification. Excess cow's milk (> 500-600 mL/day) is a common displaceable contributor. Multidisciplinary management (dietitian, paediatrician, sometimes feeding therapist) is the standard.
key_points: Define faltering growth by crossing centile lines, not by absolute weight alone
Limit cow's milk to under 500 mL/day in toddlers
Baseline workup: FBC, UEC, LFTs, iron studies, coeliac serology, TFTs, urinalysis
Multidisciplinary outpatient management first; admit if severe or unsafe
Consider coeliac, cow's milk protein allergy, CF, malabsorption, behavioural factors
explanation.pearls: Always plot weight, length, and head circumference; isolated weight faltering with preserved length suggests under-nutrition rather than syndromic short stature.
